front: Novel: the protagonist's second husband is the mayor of Eatonville, Jody Starks
back: Their Eyes Were Watching God (Zora Neale Hurston)

front: Novel: a hurricane devastates Lake Okeechobee; the protagonist shoots her husband after he is bitten by a rabid dog
back: Their Eyes Were Watching God (Zora Neale Hurston)

front: Novel: the title comes from characters staring into darkness after a wind with 'triple fury' knocks out their lights
back: Their Eyes Were Watching God (Zora Neale Hurston)

front: Work: an ethnography compiled from trips to Eatonville, Polk County Florida, and New Orleans; the author lies about being a bootlegger to collect folklore
Work: the author lies on a snakeskin for 69 hours and has '5 psychic experiences' during a hoodoo initiation
Short Story: an abusive husband named Sykes brings home a snake to scare the washerwoman Delia, but it bites him instead
Short Story: Missie May sleeps with ice cream parlor owner Otis Slemmons for his fake gold coins; her husband Joe later slips the coins under her pillow
Short Story: the sexually jealous Joe Kanty is killed for threatening his wife's lover, who then falls onto a moving saw; appeared in Alain Locke's The New Negro
Play: Methodists and Baptists argue during a trial between Jim Weston and Dave Carter over whether an object is a weapon; co-written with Langston Hughes
Author: Alice Walker placed a tombstone reading 'A Genius of the South' on this author's unmarked grave in Fort Pierce, Florida
Author: essay describes feeling the title attribute most 'when thrown against a sharp white background' and compares people to bags of miscellany filled by 'the Great Stuffer of Bags'
back: Zora Neale Hurston (How It Feels To Be Colored Me)

front: Author: interviewed Cudjoe Lewis, last survivor of the Middle Passage, in the posthumously published Barracoon (2018)
Author: met Felicia Felix-Mentor, the first photographically documented 'zombie,' while researching voodoo in Jamaica and Haiti
Author: compared anthropology to a 'spyglass'; studied under Franz Boas at Columbia; first African-American graduate of Barnard
Author: criticized Richard Wright's 'sobbing school' and rejected his approach in favor of 'sharpening my oyster knife'
Author: described the importance of 'angularity,' 'asymmetry,' and the 'will to adorn' to Black culture in The Characteristics of Negro Expression
